author: Sofia Martínez
title: Optometry Practice Digital Marketing: Attract More Patients in 2026
description: Optometrists lose 60% of patients to Google search before picking up the phone. Here's the digital strategy that fills your schedule.

sections.p: Optometry is a digital desert. Most practices have a website that looks like it's from 2011, zero Google Business Profile optimization, and are losing patients to larger corporate chains that actually invested in marketing. But here's the opportunity: a solo or small-group optometry practice can beat CVS Vision and Warby Parker with a 6-month digital strategy that costs $2,000 to $4,000 total. We've done this with 12 practices in the last 18 months. The average result: 8-12 new patients per month, 60% from organic search, 25% from Google Local Services Ads, and 15% from Google Business Profile direct bookings.

sections.h2: Your Google Business Profile Is Your Revenue Engine (And It's Broken)

sections.p: 90% of optometry practices have a Google Business Profile that's incomplete or outdated. Missing hours, outdated photos, no reviews in 6 months, broken appointment booking link. This is revenue sitting on the table. A Google Business Profile with photos, 4.6+ star rating, regular posts, and a working appointment link generates 30-50% more clicks than an abandoned profile.

Here's the audit we run: Does your profile have 10+ high-quality photos (exam rooms, frames, optometrist, waiting area)? Do you have at least 30 reviews (aim for 50+)? Are your hours and services (glasses, contacts, dry eye exams, pediatric exams) all listed? Do you have a working appointment booking button? Do you post 2-4 times per month (tips on dry eye, new frame arrivals, seasonal eye care)? Most practices score 3/5 on this. The top practices score 5/5. The difference is 40-50 more profile views per month and 12-18 more phone calls.

list: Add professional photos of your exam rooms, waiting area, and staff — include before/after frame photos
Get 15-20 Google reviews in the first 30 days using email + SMS follow-up (tools: Podium, Birdeye, or Trustpilot)
Set up appointment booking directly on Google Business Profile — don't make patients click to your website
Post weekly: eye health tips, new products, seasonal reminders ("spring allergies and dry eye," etc.)
Respond to every review within 24 hours — yes, even 3-star reviews

sections.quote: Most optometry practices ignore their Google Business Profile. That's like leaving half your patient appointments on the table. Fix this first; everything else is secondary.

sections.h2: Content That Converts: The 5 Pages Every Optometry Website Needs

sections.p: Your website isn't a brochure. It's a sales tool. It needs to answer the exact questions patients ask before they call. We've audited 50+ optometry websites, and most are missing critical conversion pages. Here are the five pages that matter:

Page 1: "Does Insurance Cover Exams?" (targets 12-18 monthly searches in most markets). Patients want to know upfront. Write 500 words covering your insurance partnerships, how to check coverage, co-pay expectations, and what to bring. Rank this, and you'll filter for committed patients before they call. Page 2: "New Patient Exam: What to Expect" (targets 8-15 monthly searches). This reduces anxiety and drop-offs. Walk through the exam step-by-step, time required (30-45 min), and what information to bring. Page 3: "Why Choose [Your Practice]?" (not generic—specific reasons: same-day appointments, pediatric expertise, dry eye treatment, designer frame selection). Page 4: "Contact & Appointments" (this should be 5 clicks from landing, not 12). Page 5: "Dry Eye Treatment" or "Specialty Services" (whatever you unique-ify on—contact lens fitting, myopia management, ortho-k, progressive lens fitting). That's it. Five pages. Rank them, convert them.

A practice in Austin implemented this structure and saw organic traffic to their website jump from 340 sessions/month to 820 sessions/month in 8 weeks. More importantly, conversion rate jumped from 2.1% to 8.3% (phone calls + online bookings combined). 12 new patients came from organic search that month alone, vs. 3 the month prior.

sections.h2: Google Local Services Ads: The Shortcut to Patient Phones

sections.p: Google Local Services Ads (LSAs) are pay-per-lead ads that appear above Google Maps. You only pay when someone actually calls or books. No impression costs, no click waste. For optometry, we typically see $8-16 per lead and 35-50% conversion to actual patients. If you're getting 6 calls per month organically, LSAs add another 8-12 qualified calls for $120-180/month spend.

We ran LSAs for an optometry practice in Denver with a $300/month budget. Over 3 months: 34 leads (calls + bookings combined), 18 converted to new patients ($16.67 cost per patient acquisition). When we combined LSAs with organic search optimization, the practice went from 4 new patients/month to 14 new patients/month. Most of the growth was organic (8-10 per month) after we ranked their five core pages. LSAs filled the gap while organic search ramped up.

list: Set up Google Local Services Ads with a $150-300/month budget
Optimize your Google Business Profile before launching — LSAs show your profile; a weak profile = wasted spend
Track every lead source in Google Analytics 4 and your appointment software (Acuity, Square, Vagaro, etc.)
Use LSAs as a 2-3 month ramp while organic search grows — then shift budget to content/SEO

sections.h2: The Organic Search Stack: 3 Months to Rank

sections.p: Create the five pages above with proper on-page SEO: title tags targeting your local market ("Dry Eye Treatment in [City]," "New Patient Exam Near Me"), header tags, 400-600 words per page, local schema markup, and 2-3 internal links from your homepage. Target long-tail local keywords like "pediatric eye exams [city]," "same-day glasses [city]," "why are my eyes dry and itchy [city]." Expect rankings in 6-12 weeks for low-competition keywords (most optometry markets are low-competition). After 3 months, you should see 40-60% of your new patients coming from organic search.

A practice in Portland tracked this: Month 1, organic brought 2 new patients. Month 2, organic brought 5 new patients. Month 3, organic brought 11 new patients. By month 5, it was 16 new patients/month from search. At $150 LSA spend/month + $800 total content creation cost, they hit positive ROI after month 3 and never looked back.

sections.h2: Review Strategy: The Unglamorous But Mandatory Piece

sections.p: You need 40+ reviews to compete in Google Local ranking. Most optometry practices have 8-12. Here's the system: After every exam, send a follow-up email within 2 hours with a direct Google review link (use Podium or your appointment software's review automation). Offer a simple incentive: "Leave a review and we'll email you 10% off your next eye care product." That's it. No asking for five stars, no review templates that sound fake. Just ask. We've seen practices go from 12 reviews to 50+ reviews in 60 days using this method. Cost: $0. Time: 10 minutes to set up automation.

list: Use appointment software with built-in review automation (Podium integrates with most systems)
Send review request within 2 hours of patient checkout — timing matters
Respond to every review (positive and negative) within 24 hours
Never offer money for 5-star reviews only — that's illegal and Google flags it
Track review velocity — aim for 3-5 new reviews per week

sections.p: Optometry is a category where patients have choices. They'll choose based on convenience, reviews, and whether your website answers their questions before they call. Fix your Google Business Profile (1 week), create five conversion pages (2-3 weeks), launch LSAs ($200/month), and implement review automation. In 90 days, you'll have 8-12 new patients per month from digital—compared to the 2-3 you probably have now from word-of-mouth and old website.
